title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a geneticist called Bella Thorne talking about the possibility of bringing extinct species back to life. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a geneticist called Bella Thorne talking about the possibility of bringing extinct species back to life. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Bella Thorne discusses the ethics and technology behind 'de-extinction' projects like the Woolly Mammoth.
extracts.audio_script:
Good morning. I’m Bella. For decades, the idea of bringing back the Woolly Mammoth or the Passenger Pigeon was the stuff of science fiction. But today, it’s becoming a scientific reality. My interest in genetics didn't start with a microscope; it actually began when I saw a [7]stuffed dodo[/7] in a museum as a teenager. I was struck by the tragedy that such a unique creature was gone forever because of human actions. I wanted to see if we could undo the damage.

De-extinction is not about making 'clones'. I’ve found that [8]gene editing[/8] is the most effective tool we have. We take the DNA of a living relative—like the Asian Elephant—and edit it to include the traits of its extinct ancestor, like thick fur or smaller ears. The result is a 'hybrid' animal that can survive in the cold tundra once again.

The goal is not just to see these animals in a zoo; it’s about restoring ecosystems. I noticed that [9]grassland restoration[/9] is the primary ecological benefit of bringing back the mammoth. These large animals act as 'ecosystem engineers'; by knocking over trees and trampling the snow, they help the soil stay frozen, which prevents the release of methane. They could be a vital tool in fighting climate change.

Technology is allowing us to sequence DNA from very old samples. I found that [10]permafrost preservation[/10] is the reason why we have such high-quality genetic material from the Ice Age. Because the animals were frozen quickly, their DNA didn't degrade as fast as it would in a warmer climate. It’s like a genetic time capsule waiting to be opened.

However, there are massive ethical concerns. I’ve found that [11]habitat loss[/11] is the biggest challenge for any de-extinction project. What is the point of bringing an animal back if it has nowhere to live? We have to ensure that the environment is ready for them before we even think about releasing them into the wild. Otherwise, we are just setting them up for a second extinction.

We also have to consider the social behavior of these animals. I’m worried that [12]lack of parenting[/12] will be a major problem for the first generation of de-extinct species. Many animals learn how to survive and communicate from their parents. Without older members of their own species to teach them, these new animals might be socially dysfunctional. It’s a psychological challenge as much as a genetic one.

Looking forward, I believe we will see the rise of 'precision conservation'. I noticed that [13]resurrection biology[/13] is already being used to help endangered species that are still alive but have very low genetic diversity. By adding 'lost' genes back into a population, we can make them more resilient to disease. It’s a way of future-proofing our current wildlife.

Finally, I’m currently writing a [14]book chapter[/14] on the legal status of de-extinct animals. Should a 'mammoth-elephant hybrid' have the same protections as an endangered species? These are the questions we need to answer before the first one is born. Thank you.
